title: practical english usage - Part 343
content:
Her husband was sitting in the front of the car. He looked frightened.

We stood in front of her car so that she couldn't drive off.

front of it you are inside. Compare: If you are in front of a place, vehicle, etc you are outside it; if you are in the 3 in   front      of   and   in   the    front      of I always find myself behind people like that.

The woman in front of me at the ticket window had a complicated problem.

In front of is often the opposite of behind.

the road from the school.) There's a bus stop opposite the school. (The bus stop is on the other side of the road as the school.) There's a bus stop in front of the school. (The bus stop is on the same side of Compare: 2 in   front      of   and   opposite «551 vocabulary           out of ►    439 and owing      to      due    to ►    354 and over        above ►    357 and over,     across          through For into and in, ►     196.

I'm afraid Mr Pallery is out of the office at the moment.

Out  of can also be used to talk about position - the opposite of in.

She turned and went out the back door. (AmE) In American English, out is normally used without oʃin this case.

Why did you throw the paper out of the window?

I walked out of the front door without looking back.

which  somebody/something goes out.

Out  of is also used to mean 'through', when we  mention the opening through I took Harry's photo out of my pocket.

not                                                  or (     She ran out the room-,  She ran out from the room.) She ran out of the room.

The  opposite of the preposition into is out of.

Why don 't you think more about other people?

Other people often means 'people besides oneself.

Have you got any other cakes, or are these the only ones?

I think we should paint it another colour.

(An)other can also mean '(an) alternative', 'besides / instead of this/these*.

For other cases where a(n) is followed by a plural, ►     130.7.

We need another three chairs.

I'm staying for another few weeks.

or a number.

However, we  can use another before a plural noun in expressions with few not Would you like some more peas? (     .. . other peas?) not Would you like some more meat? (     ..

with this meaning.

With uncountable and plural nouns, we  normally use more, not other, Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  552  own ►     ɜes and permit,       allow         let ►     525 and perhaps            maybe Jess was in Australia part of last year.

Part of the trouble is that I can't see very well. (More natural than A part of but Part of the roof was missing, (    A large part of the roof was missing.) A is usually dropped before part of if there is no adjective.

178.7.

For by oneself used in similar ways, ► Don't help him. Let him  do it on his own. (without help) My mother lives on her own. (without company) Note the two meanings of on one's own.

5 on   one's     own (xor. . . brusMwrsdfsdmir?) She can wash herself and brush her own hair now.

I'll do it myself, and I'll do it in my own way. (     ... in myself s way?) possessive forms. My own, etc is used instead.

The  emphatic and reflexive pronouns myself, yourself, etc (►   178) do not have 4 own     and   -self not (     .. . mine own?)’ ' Would you like to use my pen? 'No, thanks. I can only write with my own.

your own, etc if the meaning is clear.

We   cannot use mine, yours, etc with own, but we  can drop a noun after my own, 3   own     with    no   following       noun He's got no ideas of his own.

It's time you found some friends of your own.

I'd like to have a car of my own.

in the phrase.

This structure makes it possible to include a/an, some or another determiner 2  a/some      ...  of   one's     own I'm my own boss.

(     ... the own car.) Car rental is expensive. It's cheaper to take one's own car.

It's nice if a child can have his or her own room, (     .. .-an own room?) We   only use own after a possessive word. It cannot directly follow an article.

1  after    possessives 552  own vocabulary' •  556  please and thank you 'Do you mind if I open the window?' 'Please do.' Please do is a rather formal answer to a request for permission.
